1.title: Job sanctified his children
1.text: After their feasts, Job offered burnt offerings for each child, fearing: 'It may be that my sons have sinned, and cursed God in their hearts.'

1.question: What did Job offer for his children after their feasts?
What did Satan insinuate about Job's fear of God?
options: That Job feared God for nothing, only out of self-interest
That Job had never feared God

1.question: Who fell upon the oxen and donkeys and slew the servants?
How did Job react to losing everything?
options: 'The Lord gave, and the Lord hath taken away; blessed be the name of the Lord'
He accused the servants of lying
He fled the land of Uz

2.question: What did Satan ask the Lord in the second test?
options: To touch Job's bone and his flesh

2.question: What did Job's wife say to him?

2.title: Seven days in silence
2.text: The three friends sat with Job on the ground seven days and seven nights, and none spoke a word, for they saw that his grief was very great.

2.question: How long did the friends sit with Job without a word?

3.question: What did Job curse at the start of this chapter?

3.title: Why did I not die at birth?
3.text: Job asks why he did not die from the womb, for then he 'should have lain still and been quiet,' at rest from his affliction.

3.question: What does Job say he would do had he died at birth?
options: He would lie still and be at rest
He would reign over the earth

3.question: To what does Job compare the rest he longed for?
options: A hidden untimely birth that never sees the light

3.question: Who does Job say rejoice and are glad?
How does Job describe his state at the chapter's end?
options: He was never at rest, but trouble came upon him
He had regained all his wealth
He felt no pain at all

4.question: What is Eliphaz's argument about the innocent?
options: That no innocent person ever perished
That all the righteous die young
That suffering never has a cause

4.title: The image of the lion
4.text: Eliphaz uses the lion as a figure: the roaring and the teeth of the lions are broken, and the old lion perishes for lack of prey — so ends the wicked.

4.question: What figure does Eliphaz use for the wicked's end?
options: The lion whose teeth are broken

4.question: What came upon Eliphaz in the night vision?
options: Fear and trembling, which made his bones shake

4.question: According to the vision, how do those in houses of clay die?

5.question: What does Eliphaz say happens to the foolish man's children?
options: They are far from safety and crushed in the gate

5.title: Marvels without number
5.text: Eliphaz declares that God 'doeth great things and unsearchable; marvellous things without number'.

5.question: How does Eliphaz describe God's works?
How does God treat the wise, according to Eliphaz?
options: He takes them in their own craftiness

5.question: What paradox does Eliphaz state about God's hands?
options: He woundeth, and his hands make whole

5.question: With what shall the one God restores be in league?
options: With the stones of the field
With the kings of the earth

5.question: How does Eliphaz close his counsel to Job?
options: He says it is so and bids Job to know it for his good

6.question: What comparison does Job make to justify his crying out?
options: Even the wild donkey does not bray over fresh grass
The lion roars when it hunts
The ox rests in the stall

6.question: What consolation does Job say he would yet have?
options: That he has not concealed the words of the Holy One

6.title: The duty of a friend
6.text: Job affirms that to the afflicted, a friend should show pity, even to one who has forsaken the fear of the Almighty.

6.question: What does Job say a friend owes the afflicted?
To what does Job compare his treacherous friends?
options: To brooks that dry up and vanish

6.question: What does Job deny having asked of his friends?
options: To deliver him from the oppressor or redeem him

6.question: What question does Job ask about his tongue?
options: Whether there is iniquity in his tongue
Whether he lied to his friends

7.question: How does Job describe his flesh in this chapter?
options: Clothed with worms and clods of dust

7.title: The cloud that vanishes
7.text: Job says that, like the cloud that is consumed and vanishes, he who goes down to the grave shall return no more to his house.

7.question: To what does Job compare one who goes down to the grave?
options: The cloud that vanishes and returns not

7.question: In his anguish, what would Job's soul choose?
options: Strangling and death rather than life

7.question: What does Job ask of God at the chapter's end?
options: To pardon his transgression before he lies in the dust

8.question: What condition does Bildad set for God to restore Job?
options: That Job be pure and upright
That Job leave the land of Uz

8.title: The papyrus without mire
8.text: Bildad asks whether the rush grows without mire or the flag without water — a figure that, without God, the hypocrite's hope withers.

8.question: What figure does Bildad use for the hypocrite's hope?
options: The rush that withers without mire

8.question: To what does Bildad compare the wicked who seems prosperous?
options: To a green plant that is rooted out of its place
To a river that never dries

8.question: What does Bildad say will happen to the dwelling of the wicked?
options: It shall be rich and safe

9.question: What work of God does Job describe at the start?
options: He removes the mountains, and they know not
He creates the beasts of the field
He seals the gates of death

9.question: What constellations does Job say God made?

9.title: How to answer God?
9.text: Job asks: if even the proud helpers stoop, 'how much less shall I answer him, and choose out my words to reason with him?'

9.question: Why does Job doubt he could answer God?
options: Because even the proud helpers stoop before Him

9.question: When force is spoken of, what does Job say of God?
What complaint does Job make about the earth?
options: It is given into the hand of the wicked
It belongs only to the upright

9.question: Feeling condemned, what does Job ask?
options: Why he should labor in vain

9.question: What does Job say is lacking between him and God?
options: A daysman who might lay his hand upon them both

10.question: Why does Job ask if God has eyes of flesh?
options: To question whether God sees as man sees, searching his sin
To ask for healing of eyes

10.title: Formed like clay
10.text: Job reminds God: 'Thou hast clothed me with skin and flesh, and hast fenced me with bones and sinews' — he asks the Creator to remember whom He formed.

10.question: With what does Job remind God He clothed him?
options: Skin and flesh, bones and sinews

10.question: To what does Job compare how God hunts him?
How does Job describe the land to which he will go?
options: A land of darkness and the shadow of death, without order
A land of light and joy
A land of milk and honey
A land of rest and peace

11.question: What harsh charge does Zophar make against Job?
options: That God exacts of him less than his iniquity deserves

11.title: God knows vain men
11.text: Zophar affirms that God 'knoweth vain men: he seeth wickedness also' — nothing escapes Him, even what seems hidden.

11.question: What does Zophar say God knows and sees?
What does Zophar promise Job if he purifies himself?
options: He shall forget his misery as waters that pass away
He shall see God face to face

11.question: How does Zophar say the hope of the wicked ends?
options: It shall be as the giving up of the ghost
It shall be crowned with glory

12.question: How does Job describe the contempt of those at ease?
options: They despise the one ready to slip, like a lamp held in scorn

12.title: All life in God's hand
12.text: Job declares that in God's hand 'is the soul of every living thing, and the breath of all mankind.'

12.question: What does Job say is in God's hand?
options: The soul of every living thing

12.question: What power over the waters does Job attribute to God?
options: He withholds the waters and they dry up; he sends them and they overturn the earth
He creates the fish of the sea

12.question: What does God do to the trusted and the aged, per Job?
options: He removes the speech of the trusty and the understanding of the aged

12.question: How do the chiefs whose heart God takes away wander?
options: They grope in the dark, staggering like a drunken man

13.question: What ironic counsel does Job give his friends?
options: That they hold their peace, for it would be their wisdom

13.title: Speaking for God with deceit
13.text: Job warns his friends: God will reprove them if they secretly accept persons and speak wickedly on His behalf.

13.question: Of what does Job warn friends who speak for God?
options: That God will reprove their partiality

13.question: What declaration of trust does Job make here?
options: 'Though he slay me, yet will I trust in him'

13.question: What two things does Job ask of God before he speaks?
options: To withdraw His hand, and not let His dread terrify him
To restore his goods and health

13.question: To what does Job compare the man who wastes away?
options: To rottenness and a garment eaten by the moth

14.question: What question does Job ask about clean and unclean?
options: Who can bring a clean thing out of an unclean? Not one
Who will make the unclean a king?

14.question: What contrast does Job draw between the tree and man?
options: A felled tree sprouts again, but where is man when he dies?
Man sprouts again and the tree does not

14.title: The steps numbered
14.text: Job says to God: 'now thou numberest my steps,' feeling that his every move is watched because of his sin.

14.question: What does Job feel God does with his steps?
options: Numbers them and watches his sin

14.question: How does Job describe the state of the man who passes?
options: His flesh has pain and his soul mourns within him

15.question: Of what does Eliphaz accuse Job's mouth?
options: Of uttering his iniquity and choosing the tongue of the crafty

15.question: What authority does Eliphaz invoke against Job?
options: Among them are gray-haired men, older than Job's father
There is an angel from heaven

15.title: Not even the heavens are pure
15.text: Eliphaz affirms that God 'putteth no trust in his saints; yea, the heavens are not clean in his sight' — how much less man.

15.question: What does Eliphaz say about the heavens in God's sight?
How does Eliphaz describe the life of the wicked?
Why, according to Eliphaz, does the wicked suffer?
options: Because he stretches out his hand against God and defies Him

15.question: What will happen to the branches of the wicked?
options: The flame shall dry them up
They shall be watered by God

15.question: What, according to Eliphaz, do the hypocrites conceive?

16.question: How does Job say he would treat his friends if they were in his place?
options: He would strengthen them with his mouth and assuage their grief
He would accuse them of sin

16.question: How does Job describe the attack of those gathered against him?
options: They gaped with their mouth and smote him on the cheek in scorn

16.title: Sackcloth and dust
16.text: Job tells that he sewed sackcloth upon his skin and defiled his head in the dust — deep signs of his mourning and humiliation.

16.question: What signs of mourning does Job describe?
options: Sackcloth on his skin and his head in dust

16.question: What does Job say about the path he will soon take?
options: He shall go the way whence he shall not return

17.question: What saying does Job utter about one who flatters friends?
options: The eyes of his children shall fail
He shall be crowned with honor

17.title: No wise man among them
17.text: Job challenges his friends to return, but declares 'I cannot find one wise man among you' — he has lost hope of comfort from them.

17.question: What does Job conclude about his friends?
options: He finds no wise man among them

17.question: Where does Job say his hope shall descend?
options: To the bars of the pit, to rest in the dust

18.question: What does Bildad say happens to the light of the wicked?
options: It shall be put out, and his spark shall not shine

18.title: Hidden snares
18.text: Bildad describes a cord laid in the ground and a trap in the way — figures of how the wicked falls into his own ruin.

18.question: What figures does Bildad use for the wicked's ruin?
options: A hidden cord and a trap

18.question: What shall be scattered upon the wicked's dwelling?
How does Bildad sum up the place of the wicked?
options: It is the place of him that knoweth not God
It is the house of the righteous
It is the throne of kings
It is the garden of the blessed

19.question: What does Job say of his error, if indeed he has erred?
options: His friends will pay for it
It will be shared by all

19.question: How does Job say God has counted him?

19.title: A stranger in his own house
19.text: Job relates that his breath became strange to his wife and even his servant gave no answer — he is forsaken by all his own.

19.question: To whom did Job's breath become strange?
Why does Job beg his friends for pity?
options: Because the hand of God has touched him

19.question: What is Job's great declaration of faith in this chapter?
What warning does Job give his friends at the end?
options: That they fear the sword, for there is a judgment

20.question: How long, according to Zophar, does the joy of the wicked last?
options: It is brief, but for a moment

20.title: Restoring to the poor
20.text: Zophar says the children of the wicked shall seek to please the poor, and his own hands shall restore the goods he took.

20.question: What will the wicked's hands do, per Zophar?
What will God do with the riches the wicked swallowed?
options: He shall cast them out of his belly

20.question: What peace does Zophar say the wicked does not feel?
options: He felt no quietness in his belly

20.question: What image of judgment does Zophar use for the wicked?
options: The drawn sword comes out of his body, with terrors

20.question: How does Zophar sum up the lot of the wicked man?
options: It is the portion God appoints him as a heritage
It is the blessing of the righteous
It is the reward of the upright
It is the prize of the wise

21.question: What gesture does Job ask of his friends in their astonishment?
options: To lay their hand upon their mouth

21.question: What does Job observe about the cattle of the wicked?
options: Their bull breeds without fail and their cow does not cast her calf

21.question: What question do the wicked ask about the Almighty?
options: 'What is the Almighty, that we should serve him?'

21.question: What does Job ask about the wicked once he is dead?
options: What pleasure has he in his house when his months are cut off?

21.question: What contrast does Job draw between two men who die?
options: One dies in fullness and another in bitterness, yet both lie in the dust

21.question: What does Job say happens to the wicked in the day of destruction?
options: He is reserved and brought forth in the day of wrath

21.question: How does Job characterize his friends' consolations?
options: Vanity, for in their answers remains falsehood

22.question: Of what does Eliphaz accuse Job regarding his brothers?
options: Of taking pledges without cause and stripping the naked

22.question: Why does Eliphaz say Job is surrounded by snares?

22.title: The way of the wicked
22.text: Eliphaz warns that Job walks 'the old way which wicked men have trodden,' who were cut down out of time, their foundation overflown with a flood.

22.question: What way does Eliphaz say Job walks?
options: The old way of wicked men

22.question: What counsel does Eliphaz give Job so good may come to him?
options: To acquaint himself with God and be at peace

22.question: What does Eliphaz promise Job if he turns back?
options: He shall delight in the Almighty and lift up his face to God
He shall live a thousand years

22.question: How, per Eliphaz, will even one not innocent be delivered?
options: By the pureness of Job's hands

23.question: What would Job do if he found God's court?
options: He would lay out his cause and fill his mouth with arguments

23.question: How does Job say he will come forth after God tries him?

23.title: Not silenced by darkness
23.text: Despite his dread of God, Job affirms he was not cut off by the darkness, nor did the gloom cover his face.

23.question: What does Job affirm about the darkness around him?
options: He was not cut off by it

24.question: To what does Job compare those who go forth early to their prey?
options: To wild donkeys in the desert

24.question: What injustice does Job denounce against the poor?
options: They make the naked go without clothing and take the sheaves from the hungry
They give them bread and shelter

24.question: What does the adulterer wait for, according to Job?
options: The twilight, saying no eye shall see him

24.title: The grave and those who sinned
24.text: Job uses the figure of drought and heat consuming the snow waters: so does the grave consume those who have sinned.

24.question: To what does Job compare the grave consuming sinners?
options: Drought and heat that consume snow

24.question: How does Job close his speech?
options: Challenging anyone to prove him a liar and make his speech nothing

25.question: To what does Bildad compare man before God?

26.question: What does Job say about the dead before God?

26.title: The earth upon nothing
26.text: Job proclaims that God 'stretcheth out the north over the empty place, and hangeth the earth upon nothing' — a striking image of creative power.

26.question: Upon what does Job say God hangs the earth?
How does Job sum up all he said about God's ways?
options: These are but the edges of His ways
It is all there is to know
It is the work of men

27.question: What does Job swear about his integrity?
options: That he will not remove it from him till he dies
That he will abandon it soon
That he will sell it to his friends

27.title: Delighting in the Almighty
27.text: Job asks whether the hypocrite will delight in the Almighty and call upon God at all times — something he, Job, does even in his pain.

27.question: What does Job ask about the hypocrite?
options: Whether he will delight in the Almighty

27.question: What happens to those left of the wicked, in death?
options: They shall be buried and his widows shall not weep

27.question: What does Job say of the rich wicked when he lies down?
options: He lies down rich, but opens his eyes and is no more
He lies down and prospers more
He lies down and is crowned
He lies down in eternal peace

27.question: What gesture of scorn is made against the wicked?
options: Men clap their hands at him and hiss him out of his place

28.question: Where, according to the chapter, does bread come from?

28.title: Man's mines
28.text: The poem describes how man cuts out rivers among the rocks and his eye sees every precious thing — skill that nonetheless cannot find wisdom.

28.question: What human skill does the poem describe?
options: Cutting rivers from rocks to find precious things

28.question: Can wisdom be bought with gold?
options: No; it cannot be gotten for gold

28.question: From whom is wisdom hidden?
options: From the eyes of all living and the fowls of the air

28.question: What is the poem's conclusion about wisdom?
options: The fear of the Lord is wisdom, and to depart from evil is understanding

29.question: What does Job most value about days past?
options: That the Almighty was with him and his children around him

29.question: Whom did Job say he delivered and helped?
options: The poor that cried and the orphan with no helper

29.title: Eyes to the blind, feet to the lame
29.text: Job describes himself as 'eyes to the blind' and 'feet to the lame,' and says he broke the jaws of the wicked and plucked the prey from their teeth.

29.question: How does Job describe himself in his past glory?
options: Eyes to the blind and feet to the lame

29.question: How did men react to Job's counsel?
options: They waited in silence and gave ear to his counsel

29.question: How does Job describe himself among the men of those days?
options: As a chief and king among the troops, comforting the mourners

30.question: How were those who now mock Job treated?
options: They were driven from among men, cried after as thieves

30.question: What does Job say God did with his 'cord'?
options: He loosed it and afflicted him

30.title: The days of affliction
30.text: Job says his soul is poured out within him and that 'the days of affliction have taken hold upon me' — the pain is unceasing.

30.question: What took hold upon Job, per this lament?
Of what does Job accuse God in this lament?
options: Of having become cruel to him

30.question: What came to Job when he looked for good and light?
Into what were Job's harp and pipe turned?
options: Into mourning and the voice of those who weep

31.question: With what did Job make a covenant at the chapter's start?
options: With his eyes, not to look upon a maid

31.question: What punishment does Job invoke if he walked with vanity?
options: That another eat what he sows and his offspring be rooted out

31.question: Why did Job not despise the cause of his servant?
options: Because he would have to answer when God rose up

31.title: Clothing the needy
31.text: Job swears he never saw any perish for lack of clothing without the poor man's loins blessing him, warmed by the fleece of his sheep.

31.question: What does Job swear about those perishing for lack of clothing?
options: The poor man's loins blessed him, being warmed

31.question: Why was God's punishment a terror to Job?
options: Because he could not endure His majesty

31.question: Why would worshiping the sun or moon be a crime for Job?
options: Because he would deny the God who is above

31.question: What does Job deny having done out of fear of the multitude?
options: Keeping silent and not going out the door

32.question: Why did Elihu wait to speak?
options: Because he was younger than the others

32.title: Who is Elihu
32.text: Elihu, son of Barachel the Buzite, of the kindred of Ram, is the fourth speaker; he waited in silence while the elders searched for words.

32.question: Why does Elihu finally decide to speak?
options: Because the three are amazed and answer no more

32.question: Why does Elihu say he will not use flattery?
options: Because his Maker would soon take him away

33.question: How does Elihu say he was made?
options: The Spirit of God made him and the breath of the Almighty gave him life

33.question: What words of Job does Elihu repeat having heard him say?
options: 'I am clean without transgression; I am innocent'

33.question: How does Elihu say God speaks to men?
options: Once, yea twice, though man does not perceive it

33.title: Chastening on a bed of pain
33.text: Elihu says God also instructs man with pain upon his bed, when even bread is loathsome to him — a call to repentance.

33.question: How does Elihu say God also instructs man?
What does God say of the man when He finds a ransom for him?
options: 'Deliver him from going down to the pit'

33.question: What does God do for the soul of one who repents?
options: He delivers it from the pit, and his life shall see the light

33.question: What does Elihu offer to teach Job?
options: Wisdom, if Job will hold his peace

34.question: What saying of Job does Elihu quote here?
options: 'I am righteous, and God hath taken away my judgment'

34.question: How does Elihu say God treats each man?
options: He repays him according to his work and his way

34.title: All flesh would perish
34.text: Elihu affirms that if God gathered to Himself His spirit and breath, 'all flesh shall perish together, and man shall turn again unto dust.'

34.question: What would happen if God gathered His spirit to Himself?
options: All flesh would perish and return to dust

34.question: What does Elihu say of God's eyes?
options: They are upon the ways of every man and see all his steps
They are closed to the world

34.question: What does God do, per Elihu, knowing men's works?
options: He overturns them in the night and they are crushed

34.question: To what end does God act so, according to Elihu?
options: That the hypocrite reign no more

34.question: Of what does Elihu accuse Job at the chapter's end?
options: Of adding rebellion to his sin and multiplying words against God

35.question: Where does Elihu bid Job look?
options: To the heavens and the clouds, higher than he

35.title: Wiser than the beasts
35.text: Elihu says God 'teacheth us more than the beasts of the earth' and makes us wiser than the fowls — a privilege men forget to acknowledge.

35.question: What does Elihu say God makes us compared to beasts?
How does Elihu sum up Job's many words?
options: He opened his mouth in vain and multiplied words without knowledge

36.question: What does Elihu say about God's greatness and contempt?
options: He is mighty, yet despises no one

36.question: Why does God open men's ears?
options: For their instruction, and bids them turn from iniquity

36.title: Learning through affliction
36.text: Elihu teaches that God 'delivereth the poor in his affliction, and openeth their ears in oppression' — suffering can be instruction, not only punishment.

36.question: According to Elihu, what does God do in the affliction of the poor?
What does Elihu say about riches before God?
options: Neither gold nor all the forces of strength would deliver

36.question: What does Elihu bid Job remember?
options: To magnify God's work which men behold

36.question: What work of nature does Elihu attribute to God here?
options: The clouds that distil and drop down rain upon man

36.question: About what does Elihu say God declares His thought?
options: About the end of the world

37.question: How does Elihu describe God's voice?
options: He roars with a great voice and thunders with His majestic voice

37.title: Frost by God's breath
37.text: Elihu says that 'by the breath of God frost is given, and the breadth of the waters is straitened' — all weather obeys His word.

37.question: What is given by the breath of God, per Elihu?
What does Elihu ask Job to consider?
Why does Elihu say they cannot order their speech to God?
How does Elihu conclude about men before God?
options: Therefore they fear Him; He regards not the wise of heart

38.question: What question does the Lord ask about the earth's founding?
options: Where wast thou when I laid the foundations of the earth?

38.question: Who, according to God, shut up the sea with doors?
options: God Himself, when He clothed it with clouds

38.title: The gates of death
38.text: God asks Job whether the gates of death have been opened to him, or whether he has seen the doors of the shadow of death — bounds only the Creator knows.

38.question: About what bounds does God question Job here?
About what does God question Job in these verses?
options: The place of light and darkness

38.question: What natural phenomenon does God mention here?
options: The parting of light and the east wind

38.question: What does God ask about the ice and frost?
options: Out of whose womb came the ice? Who gendered the frost of heaven?

38.question: What does God ask about the lightnings?
options: Whether Job can send them so they go and say: Here we are

38.question: Who, according to God, prepares food for the ravens?
options: God Himself, when its young cry to Him

39.question: About which animal does God ask Job here?
options: The wild donkey, to whom He gave the wilderness for a house

39.question: What does God ask about the wild ox?
options: Whether Job can bind him with a cord in the furrow

39.title: The ostrich without fear
39.text: God describes the ostrich, which leaves her eggs in the earth and forgets the danger, because 'God hath deprived her of wisdom' — yet she scorns the horse.

39.question: Why does the ostrich forget the danger to her eggs?
options: Because God deprived her of wisdom

39.question: What animal does God describe from this verse on?
options: The horse, with its fierce snorting

39.question: How does the horse react to the trumpets in battle?
options: He says 'Ha, ha' and smells the battle afar off

39.question: What does God say about the eagle's young?
options: They suck up blood, and where the slain are, there is she

40.question: How does Job answer the Lord in this chapter?
options: 'Behold, I am vile; I will lay mine hand upon my mouth'

40.question: What question does the Lord ask to show Job's weakness?
options: Whether Job has an arm like God or thunders with a voice like His

40.title: If you can abase the proud
40.text: God challenges Job: if he can humble every proud one and tread down the wicked, then God Himself would confess that Job's own right hand could save him.

40.question: What would God confess if Job could humble the proud?
options: That Job's right hand could save him

40.question: How does the Lord describe behemoth?
options: He is the chief of the ways of God
He is the least of animals
He is a fish of the sea
He is a bird of the air

40.question: What is said about catching behemoth?
options: It is asked whether any can take him before his eyes or pierce his nose with snares
That he is easy to catch

41.question: What does the Lord ask about leviathan?
options: Whether Job would play with him as with a bird

41.question: What conclusion does God draw from leviathan's fierceness?
options: If none dares stir him up, who can stand before God?

41.title: Scales like shields
41.text: God describes leviathan's scales as his pride, each shut up as with a close seal, so joined that no air can pass between them.

41.question: What are leviathan's scales like?
options: So joined that no air passes between

41.question: What comes out of leviathan's nostrils?
options: Smoke, as out of a seething pot

41.question: How do the mighty react when leviathan raises himself?
How does leviathan react to the spear and club?
options: He counts darts as stubble and laughs at the shaking of a spear

41.question: How does the chapter describe leviathan at the end?
options: He is a king over all the children of pride
He is the least of animals

42.question: How does Job acknowledge his limitation before God?
options: He uttered what he understood not, things too wonderful for him

42.question: When did the Lord turn Job's captivity?
options: When he prayed for his friends

42.title: The twofold restoration
42.text: God gave Job twice what he had and three daughters — Jemima, Kezia and Keren-happuch — the fairest in the land; and Job lived a hundred and forty years.

42.question: How much did God give Job in the end?
